annotations.label: Bolzano's correspondence between intervals

anchor.snippet: introduced one-to-one correspondence of infinite sets, for example between the intervals

annotations.note: Mathlib proves bounded open intervals all have cardinality continuum (`Cardinal.mk_Ioo_real`), hence are equinumerous, but Bolzano's explicit interval bijection is not a named result.

annotations.label: Cardinality

anchor.snippet: developed the notion of cardinality , comparing the sizes of two sets

annotations.note: `Cardinal` is defined as the quotient of types by equinumerosity, with `Cardinal.mk` assigning each type its cardinality.

annotations.label: Uncountability of the real numbers

anchor.snippet: the set of all real numbers is uncountable

annotations.note: `Cardinal.not_countable_real` (and `instance : Uncountable ℝ`) state that the reals are not countable.

annotations.label: Cantor's diagonal argument

anchor.snippet: the more familiar proof using his diagonal argument

annotations.note: `Function.cantor_surjective` states no function `α → Set α` is surjective, the core of Cantor's diagonal argument.

annotations.label: Cantor's theorem

anchor.snippet: the size of the power set of A is strictly larger than the size of A

annotations.note: `Cardinal.cantor : a < 2 ^ a` is exactly Cantor's theorem, with the underlying diagonal argument in `Function.cantor_surjective`.

annotations.label: Transfinite numbers (cardinals and ordinals)

anchor.snippet: Cantor developed a theory of transfinite numbers , called cardinals and ordinals

annotations.note: Both `Ordinal` and `Cardinal` are fully developed, including their transfinite arithmetic.

annotations.label: Aleph numbers

anchor.snippet: His notation for the cardinal numbers was the Hebrew letter

annotations.note: `Cardinal.aleph` enumerates the infinite cardinals indexed by ordinals.

annotations.label: Omega ordinal

anchor.snippet: for the ordinals he employed the Greek letter

annotations.note: `Ordinal.omega0` is the first infinite ordinal ω, equal to the order type of ℕ.

annotations.label: Equivalence relation

anchor.snippet: equivalence relations

annotations.note: `Setoid` (and core Lean's `Equivalence`) formalize equivalence relations as reflexive, symmetric, transitive binary relations.

annotations.label: Partition of a set

anchor.snippet: partitions of sets

annotations.note: `Setoid.IsPartition` defines a partition as a collection of pairwise disjoint nonempty sets whose union is the whole type.

annotations.label: Axiom of choice

anchor.snippet: with or without the axiom of choice

annotations.note: Lean's `Classical.choice` is the axiom of choice; Mathlib's set, cardinal, and ordinal theory use classical logic with choice throughout.

annotations.label: Axiom of unrestricted comprehension (Basic Law V)

anchor.snippet: there is the set of all and only the objects that have that property

annotations.note: Mathlib uses ZFC-style separation (`ZFSet.sep`); the inconsistent unrestricted comprehension schema is deliberately not an axiom.

annotations.label: Russell's paradox

anchor.snippet: Let R be the set of all sets that are not members of themselves

annotations.note: Mathlib proves there is no universal set (`Class.univ_notMem_univ`), the consistent counterpart, but does not derive the paradox itself from naive comprehension.

annotations.label: Membership relation

anchor.snippet: a fundamental binary relation between an object o and a set A

annotations.note: `Set.Mem` gives the `Membership α (Set α)` instance providing the `∈` relation.

annotations.label: Subset (set inclusion)

anchor.snippet: If all the members of set A are also members of set B

annotations.note: `Set.Subset` defines `s₁ ⊆ s₂` as `∀ a, a ∈ s₁ → a ∈ s₂`.

annotations.label: Proper subset

anchor.snippet: We call A a proper subset of B if and only if A is a subset of B , but A is not equal to B

annotations.note: The `⊂` relation has the instance `HasSSubset (Set α)`, and `Set.ssubset_iff_subset_ne` characterizes it as `s ⊆ t ∧ s ≠ t`.

annotations.label: Union of sets

anchor.snippet: a member of A , or B , or both

annotations.note: `Set.union` is defined as `{a | a ∈ s₁ ∨ a ∈ s₂}`.

annotations.label: Intersection of sets

anchor.snippet: members of both A and B

annotations.note: `Set.inter` is defined as `{a | a ∈ s₁ ∧ a ∈ s₂}`.

annotations.label: Set difference

anchor.snippet: all members of U that are not members of A

annotations.note: `Set.diff` is defined as `{a ∈ s | a ∉ t}`.

annotations.label: Complement

anchor.snippet: is also called the complement of A in U

annotations.note: `Set.compl` is defined as `{a | a ∉ s}`, the complement within the ambient type.

annotations.label: Symmetric difference

anchor.snippet: a member of exactly one of A and B

annotations.note: `symmDiff` (notation `∆`) is defined for any generalized Boolean algebra, with sets as an instance.

annotations.label: Cartesian product

anchor.snippet: all possible ordered pairs

annotations.note: `Set.prod` (notation `×ˢ`) is the set of pairs `(a,b)` with `a ∈ s` and `b ∈ t`.

annotations.label: Empty set

anchor.snippet: the unique set containing no elements

annotations.note: The `EmptyCollection (Set α)` instance gives `∅ = {a | False}`, the set with no members.

annotations.label: Power set

anchor.snippet: the set whose members are all of the possible subsets of A

annotations.note: `Set.powerset s = {t | t ⊆ s}`.

annotations.label: Pure set

anchor.snippet: A set is pure if all of its members are sets

annotations.note: Mathlib's `ZFSet`/`PSet` cumulative hierarchy consists entirely of pure sets (it has no urelements), but no explicit `IsPure` predicate is defined.

annotations.label: Rank in the cumulative hierarchy

anchor.snippet: defined to be the least ordinal that is strictly greater than the rank of any of its elements

annotations.note: `ZFSet.rank` is defined as `⨆ y ∈ x, succ (rank y)`, exactly the least ordinal strictly above the ranks of its elements.

annotations.label: Urelements

anchor.snippet: objects that can be members of sets but that are not themselves sets and do not have any members

annotations.note: Mathlib's ZFC (`ZFSet`) is a theory of pure sets with no urelements, so the concept is not formalized.

annotations.label: Descriptive set theory

anchor.snippet: the study of subsets of the real line and, more generally, subsets of Polish spaces

annotations.note: Polish spaces (and related Borel/analytic-set machinery) are formalized, but descriptive set theory is a field rather than a single declaration.

annotations.label: Borel sets

anchor.snippet: Many properties of Borel sets can be established in ZFC

annotations.note: `MeasurableSpace.borel` is the smallest σ-algebra containing the open sets of a topological space.

annotations.label: Fuzzy set membership

anchor.snippet: an object has a degree of membership in a set, a number between 0 and 1

annotations.note: Fuzzy sets and graded membership are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Degree of membership in 'tall people'

anchor.snippet: can be a real number such as 0.75

annotations.note: This is an illustrative fuzzy-set example, and fuzzy set theory is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Inner model of ZF

anchor.snippet: a transitive class that includes all the ordinals and satisfies all the axioms of ZF

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Class` and transitivity notions but no formalization of inner models satisfying ZF.

annotations.label: Constructible universe L

anchor.snippet: The canonical example is the constructible universe L developed by Gödel

annotations.note: Gödel's constructible universe L is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Large cardinal

anchor.snippet: A large cardinal is a cardinal number with an extra property

annotations.note: Specific large-cardinal notions such as `Cardinal.IsInaccessible` (and `IsStrongLimit`, `IsRegular`) are defined, but there is no general definition of 'large cardinal'.

annotations.label: Determinacy

anchor.snippet: certain two-player games of perfect information are determined from the start

annotations.note: Combinatorial games (`SetTheory.Game`) exist, but determinacy of Gale–Stewart/infinite perfect-information games is not formalized.

annotations.label: Axiom of determinacy implies regularity of reals

anchor.snippet: AD implies that all subsets of the real line are well behaved

annotations.note: The axiom of determinacy and its consequences for the reals are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Forcing

anchor.snippet: Forcing adjoins to some given model of set theory additional sets in order to create a larger model

annotations.note: Forcing and the construction of generic extensions are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cardinal invariant

anchor.snippet: A cardinal invariant is a property of the real line measured by a cardinal number

annotations.note: Cardinal invariants of the continuum are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Set-theoretic topology

anchor.snippet: studies questions of general topology that are set-theoretic in nature

annotations.note: While general topology is extensively formalized, 'set-theoretic topology' is a research field, not a declaration.

annotations.label: Combinatorial set theory

anchor.snippet: concerns extensions of finite combinatorics to infinite sets

annotations.note: Combinatorial set theory is a field rather than a single declaration, and its core results are largely not formalized.

annotations.label: Natural numbers

anchor.snippet: of natural numbers

annotations.note: `Nat` (notation `ℕ`) is the type of natural numbers.

annotations.label: Integers

anchor.snippet: of integers

annotations.note: `Int` (notation `ℤ`) is the type of integers, constructed from `Nat`.

annotations.label: Real numbers

anchor.snippet: of real numbers

annotations.note: `Real` (notation `ℝ`) is the type of real numbers, defined as a quotient of Cauchy sequences of rationals.

annotations.label: Function as a relation between sets

anchor.snippet: defining a mathematical function as a relation from one set

annotations.note: Relations between types are formalized as `Rel`, but Mathlib treats functions as primitive type-theoretic maps (`α → β`) rather than defining them as functional relations.
